response:
for each element in the array we need to compute the sum of absolute differences between that element and all others except itself. however for every third element we compute the sum of squares of the differences instead. given that the array is sorted we can leverage this property to compute the sums efficiently. for the absolute difference part we can use prefix sums to calculate the total contribution of elements before and after the current index. for the square difference part we need to calculate the sum of squares of differences which can be done using the formula involving the sum of squares and the square of the sum. this ensures that the solution is efficient and works within the constraints.

***

the approach involves iterating through the array and for each index calculating the appropriate sum. for indices that are not multiples of three we use the prefix sum technique to calculate the absolute difference sum in constant time. for indices that are multiples of three we compute the sum of squares of differences using the formula sum((nums[i] - nums[j])^2) which expands to nums[i]^2 * n - 2 * nums[i] * sum(nums) + sum(nums^2). this method ensures that the solution runs in linear time which is essential for handling large input sizes as specified in the constraints. the final result array is built accordingly and returned.
